cataloging_subject.label: Life of Saint Brendan
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual unit is the anonymous Middle English verse Life of Saint Brendan transmitted within the South English Legendary. CME00141 represents Martha Bälz's 1909 twelve-witness critical edition: an Ashmole 43 running base text, normally reached through Horstmann 1874, with readings from eleven comparison witnesses reached through printed editions, Arnold Hahn's manuscript copies, and one unresolved Bodley 779 route. Bälz's German title page and embedded critical apparatus are modeled as editorial content, not as a second medieval work. The record does not treat the whole South English Legendary, all seventeen DIMEV witnesses, the dissertation's omitted introduction, or any physical codex as if it were the work itself.

catalog_summary.title: Life of Saint Brendan
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English author or adapter
catalog_summary.date: Composed 1250-1290, with circa 1260 suggested; extant by the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century; Bälz edition published 1909
catalog_summary.region: England; the original dialect is only broadly southern and no precise place of composition is established

titles.supplied_by: modern cataloging title aligned with DIMEV and current manuscript catalogs

assertion.notes: A supplied uniform work title, not a claim that every witness carries this exact heading.

titles.value: Die me. Brendanlegende des Gloucesterlegendars: kritisch herausgegeben mit Einleitung

scope.description: The 1909 edited representation rather than a medieval title

titles.value: Seyn Brendan, þe holi mon, was of Irlonde
titles.supplied_by: Bälz's edited text as encoded in CME00141

assertion.notes: The Bälz-edition reading; DIMEV uses a normalized indexing form and witness spellings vary.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English author or adapter
notes: The identity is unknown. Saint Brendan is the subject of the legend, not its author.
The poem probably adapts the Latin Navigatio Sancti Brendani tradition, but no physical exemplar or named translator is established.

agents.name: Martha Bälz
notes: Editor and collator of the twelve-witness 1909 critical representation encoded as CME00141.

agents.name: Arnold Hahn
notes: Bälz credits Hahn with manuscript copies for sigla C, A, L, R, G, O, T, and I; the survival and present location of those copies remain unresolved.

assertion.notes: No named medieval author is supported by the evidence inspected.

scope.description: Probable adaptation of Latin Navigatio material into the South English Legendary form

assertion.notes: The precise Latin recension, exemplar, and mechanics of adaptation remain unresolved.

scope.description: The 1909 edited representation, including running text, title page, and critical apparatus
Ashmole 43 running base text, with declared critical departures

assertion.notes: The supported Ashmole route is mediated by Horstmann 1874; Bälz records seven explicit base-text changes and additional stated emendation criteria.

scope.description: Twelve-witness apparatus using Bälz's sigla M, H, W, B, C, A, L, R, G, O, T, and I

assertion.notes: The lineage record preserves the different consultation routes instead of treating all twelve witnesses as direct autopsy.

scope.description: Copy intermediaries for Bälz sigla C, A, L, R, G, O, T, and I

assertion.notes: This responsibility describes lost or unlocated copy intermediaries, not transcription of CME00141 itself.
A specialist composition range for the abstract poem; it is not inferred from the date of any surviving witness.

date_statements.display: Extant by circa 1300 in overlapping early witnesses, including Laud Misc. 108 and Harley MS 2277

scope.description: Joint qualified attestation window among the seventeen witnesses researched

assertion.notes: Laud Misc. 108's target belongs to an early core within a codex whose broader history extends later, while Harley MS 2277 is dated circa 1300 (1295-1305). Ashmole 43 and Egerton 2891 also overlap the opening of the fourteenth century. The evidence does not establish which surviving witness is earliest, so no singular lineage entity is asserted. Bälz's 1909 date remains on the edition layer; it is not the work's first publication because earlier witness editions appeared in 1844 and 1874.

places.label: England
notes: Used only for the work's broad cultural origin.

places.label: Southern England
notes: A deliberately broad language-region label; PCMEP says the original dialect is unknown but rules out northern origin.

assertion.notes: The work is Middle English and part of the South English Legendary tradition; a more precise place of composition is not established.

scope.description: Reconstructed original language, not any one witness dialect

assertion.notes: PCMEP reports that the original dialect is unknown, though northern origin can be ruled out. Bälz's Gloucester framing belongs to an older scholarly argument and is not adopted as a firm composition place.

scope.description: Work-level form; the separately modeled editorial apparatus does not make the medieval work mixed

form_statements.description: A Middle English poem in rhyming aa couplets, commonly described as septenary with seven stresses and two half-lines.
German prose framing and critical notation containing Middle English witness readings.

assertion.notes: A descriptive narrative mode within the saints' life; not used here to reclassify the poem as secular romance.
Lavery emphasizes the plain vernacular didactic and homiletic manner of the South English Legendary adaptation.

subject_statements.label: Terrestrial Paradise and the Land of Promise

summaries.value: A Middle English verse life recounting Brendan's sacred sea voyage with a company of monks. The voyagers pass providential and marvelous islands, encounter the whale Jasconius, birds associated with fallen angels, demons and Judas, hermits, and paradisal spaces, and ultimately reach the Land of Promise before Brendan returns and dies. The narrative adapts hagiographic voyage material to the concise didactic and homiletic manner of the South English Legendary.

CME00141 contains Bälz's German thesis title page and one 738-line Middle English poem in a single verse group, with 771 embedded critical notes. The XML has two DIV1 elements, one LG, 71 page breaks, five title-page paragraphs, and no subsidiary DIV2 sections. The cached source preserves the title page and printed pp. 1-70, the complete boundary encoded by CME00141, but not the dissertation's xxxii-page introduction.

The running text is based on Bälz's M, Ashmole 43, with eleven comparison witnesses. The supported routes are Horstmann 1874 for M; Horstmann 1887 for H; Wright 1844 for W; a probable Horstmann 1874 route for B, whose direct consultation remains unresolved; and Arnold Hahn's copies for C, A, L, R, G, O, T, and I. DIMEV lists five additional witnesses that belong to the wider tradition but are not sources of Bälz's edition.

Work, witness, edition, intermediary, facsimile, and encoding layers are kept separate. The thirteenth-century composition range belongs to the abstract poem; dates, regions, and dialects assigned to individual codices describe those witnesses; 1909 describes Bälz's publication; and the 738-line and 771-note counts describe CME00141's edited digital representation. Bälz's historical Gloucester localization is therefore not transferred to the abstract work as a confirmed place of composition.

extent.description: The cataloged unit contains one medieval verse work plus editorial framing and apparatus. The XML has two DIV1 elements, no DIV2 or HEAD, one LG, 738 L, 771 NOTE, 71 PB, no MILESTONE, five P, 7,459 HI, one revision ITEM, and no ITEM1. The revision ITEM is header metadata, not work content. Bälz and DIMEV report 738 lines; Lavery reports 734, PCMEP reports 732 plus final Amen, and CME00107 has 733 main XML lines. Those measures remain explicitly unresolved.

content_parts.label: Life of Saint Brendan
content_parts.summary: The anonymous Middle English verse saints' life, represented by Bälz's critically edited Ashmole-based running text.

locators.value: running poem and apparatus on pp. 1-70

notes: The 738 count is the Bälz-edition XML stream. It is not silently reconciled with Lavery's 734, PCMEP's 732 plus Amen, or CME00107's 733 main lines.
The eleven comparison-witness readings are apparatus for this work, not additional content works.

content_parts.label: Bälz's title-page framing and critical apparatus
content_parts.summary: German thesis framing and 771 embedded critical notes recording readings from the M/H/W/B/C/A/L/R/G/O/T/I witness set.

locators.value: unnumbered title page and apparatus on printed pp. 1-70

notes: The 771 NOTE elements are textual apparatus entries, not 771 independent works or sections.
The dissertation's xxxii-page German introduction is outside CME00141 and outside the cached selection.

related_works.title: South English Legendary
notes: The Life circulates as one component of a fluid multi-witness saints' legend compilation; component order and textual state vary among codices.

related_works.title: Navigatio Sancti Brendani
notes: Lavery argues that the Latin Navigatio is probably the poem's principal source. This is a work-level relationship; no physical exemplar or precise recension is identified.

related_works.title: Voyage of Saint Brendan by Benedeit
notes: Bälz proposed a close French verse source relationship, while Lavery rejects direct use of Benedeit's Anglo-Norman Voyage as a secondary source. The conflict remains explicit.

related_works.title: Life of Saint Brendan (CME00107, Horstmann 1874 representation)
notes: CME00107 and CME00141 are separate edition-based digital representations of the same abstract Middle English poem. CME00107 follows Horstmann 1874; CME00141 follows Bälz 1909.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00141.xml, at CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for the CME identifiers, Bälz title and editorship, promotion date, UMDL handle, Middle English and German layers, complete encoded poem and apparatus, printed-page mapping, narrative contents, and structural boundary.

evidence.locator: DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, LG, L, NOTE, PB, MILESTONE, P, HI, ITEM, and ITEM1 counts
evidence.summary: The XML has two DIV1 elements, no DIV2 or HEAD, one LG, 738 L, 771 NOTE, 71 PB, no MILESTONE, five P, 7,459 HI, one revision ITEM, and no ITEM1. The 738 lines form one complete poem and the notes are apparatus rather than additional works.

evidence.citation: Martha Bälz, Die me. Brendanlegende des Gloucesterlegendars: kritisch herausgegeben mit Einleitung (Berlin: Mayer & Müller, 1909), title page and printed pp. 1-70.
evidence.locator: PDF pp. 1-71; title page and printed pp. 1-70
evidence.summary: Primary edition evidence for the exact text and apparatus encoded in CME00141, the 738-line numbering, title and editorship, and the fact that the local selection omits the dissertation's xxxii-page introduction.

evidence.citation: Martha Bälz, Die me. Brendanlegende des Gloucesterlegendars (1909), preliminary pp. i-v, inspected in the free Google Books viewer.
evidence.summary: Expands Bälz's sigla and consultation routes, states the base-text and emendation method, and advances historical Latin and French source arguments. The automated PDF route presented a verification challenge and was not bypassed.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 4567, IMEV/NIMEV 2868, version 1.0 data release.
evidence.locator: record 4567 and seventeen linked witness entries
evidence.summary: Supports the normalized incipit, title, DIMEV and IMEV identifiers, saints' life subject, aa-couplet form, 738-line catalog extent, and a seventeen-witness census five witnesses larger than Bälz's source set.

evidence.citation: PCMEP, text details for Saint Brendan.
evidence.locator: composition, dialect, verse form, DIMEV/IMEV identifiers, and line count
evidence.summary: Supports the 1250-1290 composition range with circa 1260 suggested, a broad southern rather than northern original dialect, septenary aa-couplet form, and a 733-line convention described as 732 lines plus final Amen.

evidence.citation: Simon Lavery, 'The Source of the St Brendan Story in the South English Legendary,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 15 (1984): 21-32.
evidence.summary: Supports probable principal dependence on the Latin Navigatio, rejects direct dependence on Benedeit's Anglo-Norman Voyage, and describes the poem's abridged vernacular didactic and homiletic hagiographic character; Lavery reports 734 lines.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Ashmole 43.
evidence.summary: Supports the Ashmole work identity, folio boundary, 1300-1325 date, and base-witness distinction; its witness properties are not transferred to the abstract work.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Laud Misc. 108.
evidence.locator: surviving Life of St Brendan, ff. 104r-110r
evidence.summary: Supports the surviving Laud boundary, physical lacuna, broad codex history, and target's position in the manuscript's early core; used cautiously as an early attestation rather than a composition date.

evidence.citation: General Corpus descriptive work-metadata manifest for CME00107.
evidence.locator: cataloging_subject, extent, content_parts, related_works, and lineage
evidence.summary: Establishes CME00107 as Horstmann's 1874 edition-based representation of the same abstract Middle English Life and preserves its distinct 733-main-line XML count.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00141.
evidence.locator: entities, relations, access, rights, editorial_practices, evidence, and open_questions
evidence.summary: Binds the work metadata to the repository XML, U-M encoding, Bälz edition, printed and copy intermediaries, twelve immediate witnesses, five collateral DIMEV witnesses, available facsimiles, access routes, and source studies without conflating their layers.

open_questions.question: How should Bälz's and DIMEV's 738 lines, Lavery's 734 lines, PCMEP's 732 lines plus final Amen, and CME00107's 733 main XML lines be reconciled across witnesses and editorial conventions?
next_steps: Collate lineation in Bälz, Horstmann, representative manuscripts, and a current critical edition.
Record separately whether Amen, rubrics, split lines, lacunae, and witness-specific additions are counted.
Retain 738 only as the Bälz/CME00141 line count until reconciled.

open_questions.question: Which Latin Navigatio recension or exemplar most directly underlies the poem, and how should Bälz's tentative French-source proposal be reconciled with Lavery's rejection of direct dependence on Benedeit's Voyage?
next_steps: Consult scholarship published after Lavery's 1984 study.
Compare distinctive narrative and verbal readings against Latin and Anglo-Norman textual traditions.
Keep work-level hypotheses separate from any claim about an identified physical exemplar.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's place and dialect of composition be localized more closely than broadly southern England without transferring properties from later witnesses or adopting Bälz's Gloucester argument uncritically?
next_steps: Consult current linguistic and South English Legendary scholarship on reconstructed archetypal language.
Separate original-language claims from the local profiles of individual witnesses.
Retain England as broad cultural origin and southern England as tentative only.

open_questions.question: Do Arnold Hahn's copy intermediaries survive, and did Bälz consult Bodley 779 directly rather than through Horstmann 1874?
next_steps: Search Bälz and Hahn archival papers and correspondence.
Compare Bälz's B apparatus with Horstmann 1874 and Bodley 779.
Retain the currently supported intermediary routes until direct consultation is evidenced.

open_questions.question: Can a stable complete copy of Bälz 1909, including all thirty-two preliminary pages, be acquired without bypassing provider verification controls?
next_steps: Request a complete scan through a holding library or authenticated provider workflow.
Retain the present local PDF as complete only for its title-page-and-pp. 1-70 selection boundary.
Recheck the preliminary source and method arguments against the complete dissertation when obtained.

notes: The preferred title is a modern supplied work title; Bälz's German dissertation title and edited incipit remain separately identified.
Saint Brendan is the subject, not an attributed author. The medieval author or adapter is anonymous.
German belongs to Bälz's editorial framing and apparatus and is not exposed in the catalog summary as an intrinsic language of the medieval work.
The main work is verse. The mixed editorial-apparatus part does not change the work-level form to mixed.
Romance is not assigned as a primary genre: the voyage contains adventure and marvels, but the strongest grounded classification is hagiographic saints' legend and religious poetry.
Bälz's twelve-source edition is distinguished from DIMEV's seventeen-witness census; the five later collateral witnesses are related sources, not retroactive sources of the 1909 edition.
CME00141 and CME00107 represent the same abstract poem through different scholarly editions and retain their distinct edited lineation and apparatus boundaries.

The record remains partial because line-count conventions, source relationships, original linguistic localization, the Hahn/Bodley consultation routes, and access to Bälz's complete preliminaries remain unresolved.
